turns.content:
How do they communicate?
Gavin is non-speaking. He can make sounds but he can't say words. It's part of his disability. He will express himself with his voice. Sometimes he's kind of singing and sometimes you hear angry noises or happy noises. He can label things with his device. Ask for help. Tell us if he is happy or sad. Gavin uses an AAC device to communicate. It's on an iPad and the software is touch chat. He uses his iPad to ask for help, like for example, if he can't open his water bottle to fill it, but sometimes he can't open it. He has low muscle tone. He'll ask for help and um he also uses it for when his iPad isn't working, he might select I want iPad. That means maybe the internet is down or maybe he can't find the video that he's actually looking for. So in that instance, I go to his search history and that usually helps him find what he's looking for.

Are there things they say or do that mean something specific? What do they mean?
If he's upset, sometimes he just wants to go on a car ride, so he'll go to his iPad device and select the word car. That means he wants to go on a trip. Usually places he likes to go are IKEA or Bass Pro Shops in Foxborough. Um, yeah. So usually he's agitated and going for a car ride really soothes him. Um, going for walks soothes him too, but right now that's a safety issue going for walks. You definitely would need more than one person to go with him, both in the car or on walks. You need at least two people just for safety reasons.

When Gavin also wants to express himself, he'll come up to you and maybe touch you or try to lead you to what he wants. Um, like maybe if something isn't working or he needs you to open something, he'll lead you to it. Um, he'll also let you know when he wants attention because he'll sit next to you and kind of get closer and closer until you start giving him attention. So that's his way of letting you know that he wants attention.

Gavin needs does Gavin does not communicate when he needs the bathroom. Um he will he does void in the toilet, but he does not have bowel movements in the toilet. He only does that in his pull up. And he does that by hiding and then you hear him grunt, usually hiding behind furniture or curtain. That's when he's doing it.

So, Gavin needs to be told to use the bathroom. He won't use it independently. The house has speakers that go off every hour and tell him to go use the bathroom. For the most part, he will go use the bathroom when they go off. Sometimes he won't because he wants to be doing something else, so you have to just remind him to go use the bathroom.

You know if when Gavin is hungry, first of all he needs food constantly. And if any of his food runs out, he starts going to the fridge often and grabbing cheese. So that's basically a sign that he's hungry and he needs more food.

When Gavin is upset, he, well, run away, he'll elope. Um, he'll definitely make angry sounds kind of like yelling as well. And sometimes if he's really dysregulated, he'll start biting his hand, which is not great. Do not try to stop him from biting his hand because then he'll just bite you. So, um, we'll go over that later. It's more about redirecting, but yeah, that's how you know he's really upset.

What helps you communicate with them?
Gavin is very visual, so it helps if you show him things to pick from, whether it's pictures of those items or the items themselves.
Gavin is also good with two-step actions, so for example, first this, then that, that helps him. If you tell him more than two steps at a time, he kind of gets lost.
Body language and communicating non-verbally also works for him. He does it, you end up learning his cues, like he'll walk in the direction he wants to go. As the caregiver, you kind of do the same thing. You, let's say if you want him to lift his foot to help him put his pants on, you just tap his foot really gently, that would work, and he knows what you mean.

How can you tell when they need help, and what should you check first?
The easiest way to tell he needs help is when he goes up to his device and presses help. That is the most straightforward way.

Are there any allergies?
no allergies

Do they have any health conditions?
Autism Spectrum Disorder: diagnosed 4/26/2015 by Dr. Catherine Davis
Cerebral Visual Impairment (CVI): diagnosed 6/26/2024 by Dr. Jam Martin
Pica: diagnosed 5/9/2025 by Dr. Patricia Peters
Language Regression: diagnosed 4/26/2015 by Dr. Catherine Davis
Mixed receptive-expressive language disorder: diagnosed 6/13/2015 by Dr. Catherine Davis
Sensory Processing Difficulty: diagnosed 6/13/2017 by Dr. Catherine Davis
Global Developmental Delay: diagnosed 7/8/2018 by Dr. Catherine Davis
Apraxia of Speech:  diagnosed 7/8/2018 by Dr. Catherine Davis
Note, his half uncle has severe autism and Tubular Sclerosis

Do they take any medication? What should others know?
ARIPiprazole 15mg (also known as Abilify) once a day at 3pm. To help manage irritability, aggression, repetitive behaviors, and self-injury.Polythylene glycol 3350 oral powder for reconstitution 17g oral (also known as ClearLax, GaviLAX, HealthyLax, MiraLax, and others). To keep stool regular.
L’il Critters Gummy Vites Daily Multivitamin for Kids dose 2 gummies per day.

Do they use any equipment or supports?
Noise cancelling headphones. Buckle Buddy for seat belt car safety. Fidgets (always needs something in his hand). Pullups. White Cane that he is currently learning how to use.

What is their typical morning routine?
On a weekday, I wake Gavin up at 7:20 a.m. Um, usually what I do is I bring the iPad to his room and I click um a video and hit play and eventually I'll let him wake up to the iPad. And while he's doing that, I give him like five minutes. I grab his clothes for the day. and then I tell him it's time to get ready for school. The van is coming.
He will go to the bathroom. One thing, we will go to the bathroom together. I'll prompt him to take his PJs off. He'll pull down his pull-up, and he'll use the toilet. I always prompt him to tuck and close his legs. Sometimes he remembers, sometimes he doesn't, because he sits when he uses the bathroom.
If Gavin does not void, sometimes he doesn't when he's being rushed in the morning, the only other way I can get him to void is to take him to the shower. He doesn't actually shower, we just turn on the showerhead and he holds it and eventually he will pee and we step out. First we put deoderant on. I do it for him. He will lift his arms for you. and then we get dressed.
When Gavin goes to school, he does wear underwear, so we put the shirt on, underwear, pants, and then we head to the family room. Once we're in the family room, I put his socks on. We brush his teeth while he's sitting on his chair, and I comb his hair as well.
Gavin does not eat breakfast on weekdays when he has school. We just wait for the van. It typically comes around 8:05 a.m. When the van comes, I say, Gavin, the van is here. He gets up. He puts his shoes on. Sometimes he can do it on his own. Sometimes he needs help. If it's cold out, we, of course, put a jacket on. And we go out to the van. I bring his backpack, his school backpack, and we buckle him in with the buckle buddy, and he is good to go.
On days when he doesn't have school it's the same routine except he will eat breakfast.

What are meals and snacks like?
For school lunches, I pack Gavin pasta with just a little bit of olive oil and Parmesan cheese sprinkled on top. I also give him a big container of cauliflower that's raw, so raw cauliflower, he really likes that. I also give him water, because he only drinks water, he doesn't drink any other liquids. And I put three teaspoons of Miralax in the water.
Gavin isn't great with cups of water, so we usually give him a sippy cup to drink his water.
Gavin has a really limited diet, unfortunately. He used to eat more, but not anymore. Lately, he only eats the following: pita bread with labneh, which is like a yogurt cream cheese spread, sprinkled with zatar herbs on top and cut into bite-sized pieces. All of Gavin's food needs to be bite-sized pieces because he has trouble biting them and he usually eats with his hands.
As I mentioned before, he also loves raw cauliflower. He likes steamed green beans. He loves any kind of lettuce, whether it's spring, romaine, just plain, nothing on it. He just likes to eat it straight.
All of those foods I just mentioned have to be prepped for him. However, we do have slices of cheddar cheese that we get from Wegmans in the fridge for him.
Gavin is able to independently open the fridge and get cheese for himself, which he does throughout the day.
Gavin does not sit down to eat at home. He is constantly walking back and forth, so we put the food up on the counter, just in front of the sink, and he literally just grazes walking by all day.
Gavin is able to fill his own water bottle, which is great. He does it through the fridge water dispenser.
When Gavin gets home from school, he likes to have one mini cupcake, which he gets himself, and one slice of cheese, which he gets himself. Gavin, in general, loves candy, sweets. He likes cake and frosting. He does not like ice cream because it's cold and slimy.
When we go to ice cream shops, we get him sprinkles because he does not like ice cream, he loves sprinkles.

What helps with transitions during the day?
For the most part, Gavin is really good at transitions. We just tell him, okay, it's time to go to the store, go to the bathroom, whatever the activity is. If he is having a hard time it's best to back off and give him some space and time before you try again.
If he really doesn't want to do the transition, using Swedish fish or any sort of gummy to reward him helps motivate him to do it. When doing tasks or asked to wait, a Visual timer most often works with Gavin. Also a visual schedule with a preferred at the end helps him get through tasks (like mom or ipad).

What do they like to do during the day?
Overall Interests include Farms Animals, Dinosaurs, Cars, trucks, Books, planets.
Going on walks, Scooter, Horseback Riding, Swimming, jumping, crashing, swinging, swimming, obstacle courses, bowling, stimming on YouTube, videos, listening to music, playing chase, sensory seeker, loves to laugh, loves tickles, basketball, guitar, drums and piano. For sensory likes mouthing spiral necklaces.
Cars/trucks/ramps, cause and effect toys, Puzzles, Trampoline, Pop tubes, Therapy ball or any ball, Swing, Roller Racer scooter, Sensory bins.

What do they enjoy doing outside the home?
He especially loves going to horseback riding lessons. He loves novelty, going to new places, exploring. He really likes walking around. He likes to keep moving and not staying still. Walking hikes, malls, stores, museums.

What activities do they enjoy most?
Gavin loves his ipad the most. He loves music. Going on car rides, walking around new places. Horseback riding. Swimming.

Who do they enjoy spending time with?
Mom is his favorite person. He also enjoys spending time with family.

What does quiet or downtime look like for them?
Downtime for Gavin is being left alone to do his own thing. Sometimes he likes to sit on the couch and watch tv.

What changes in plans or routine tend to upset or overwhelm them?
Gavin doesn't like it when things are out of place, when things are not in the place they're supposed to be, if they're moved, that upsets him.
Gavin is obsessed with lights. He likes to turn them on and off.
Gavin is also obsessed with shades being up or down. He's very rigid about if them being either up or staying down.
For lighting at home, we have to use soft indirect lighting because he won't let us turn on the overhead lighting.
When things get too loud or chaotic, it can be overwhelming for him and overstimulating, and it can be upsetting.
Gavin is very sensitive to noise and crowds.

What places or things around them can feel overwhelming?
loud noise, bright lights, crowded places, too many people.

What things like hunger, tiredness, or not feeling well can affect them?
Always hungry, so he can get cranky if he doesn't have food with him all the time. I think it's because he's a teenager and he's growing.

What signs in their body show they need help?
Doesn't let you know if he's hurt or in pain. It's very rare, so if he's walking strangely, like limping or avoiding certain body parts, check him. There might be something wrong.
If Gavin's appetite changes or so if he's not eating or not drinking, it could be because he's sick.
When Gavin is lethargic, that is definitely a sign of sickness because he usually has a lot of energy and can't sit still.

What changes in their behavior show they need help?
If Gavin disappears, it's probably because he's having a bowel movement and looking for privacy.
If Gavin is starting to get agitated, he's too dysregulated to ask for help and probably is overwhelmed and needs support.

What changes in how they communicate show they need help?
He will sign for help when he needs help. He will also go to his iPad and use the word help on his iPad, his AAC device. Other signs he needs help is, again, can be physical. He is favoring a body part or avoiding a body part, limping, and not eating.

What changes to the environment help?
If Gavin is really upset, giving him space and making sure everything is quiet and there isn't a lot going around is helpful to him. He needs to just have a moment to himself. Going up to him actually might make it worse, especially if he's doing self-harm or eloping.
Definitely leaving the house, going for a car ride helps redirect him and helps him snap out of the bad mood. Also, giving him candy like Swedish fish or gummies helps sometimes calm him and redirect him.

What calming items help them?
Gavin has some calming techniques. You could tell him to squeeze and release his hands. That's fisting his hands and opening them. So tell him squeeze, release. That usually helps. Telling him to take a deep breath or asking him to count to 10 will also help him, but this is only if he's not, if he's still sort of calm and hasn't really gotten really upset yet.
If he's really upset, again, giving him space is the best thing and giving him time to calm down.

What can you do in the moment to help?
The most important thing to do is make sure Gavin is safe. Make sure that he can't hurt himself and just give him space.

Emergency contacts:
• Rania Kelly, Mother with physical custody, 617-538-4056, 20 Blue Hill Dr, Westwood MA 02090, raniadesign@gmail.com
• Labebe Awde, Grandmother, 617-930-7229, 20 Blue Hill Dr, Westwood MA 02090, labebea@yahoo.com

sectionTitles: What helps the day go well
What can upset or overwhelm them
What helps when they are having a hard time

Daily Needs & Routines.label: toileting routine
anyOf: does not independently communicate toileting needs

bannedPhrases: what changes in their body show they need help

Communication: bowel movements happen in his pull-up
